walkthrough.title: Choose Your Palace Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get ready to build some gorgeous stained glass windows. Each player takes a palace board and eight palace strips. Each strip is double-sided, with an A side and a B side showing different color patterns. For your first game, let's all use the A sides so everyone has the same layout.

walkthrough.title: Arrange Your Strips
walkthrough.text: Let's slide all eight strips into your palace board from left to right, numbered one through eight. Each strip has spaces for colored glass panes and shows which colors can be placed there. Take a moment to look at the color requirements on each strip so you know what you will need.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Glazier Pawn
walkthrough.text: Let's place your glazier pawn on your leftmost strip. The glazier is your master artisan who moves from strip to strip placing glass panes. Remember, the glazier can only move to the right during a round, never to the left.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Factory Displays
walkthrough.text: Let's place the factory displays in the center of the table. For two players use five factories, for three players use seven, and for four players use nine. These circular displays are where you will draft your glass panes from each round.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Tile Bag
walkthrough.text: Let's put all one hundred glass pane tiles into the cloth bag and mix them up. There are five colors of panes with twenty of each color. Give the bag a good shake to make sure everything is thoroughly mixed.

walkthrough.title: Stock the Factories
walkthrough.text: Let's draw four random panes from the bag and place them on each factory display. Every factory gets exactly four tiles. The leftover panes stay in the bag for future rounds.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Center and Floor Line
walkthrough.text: Let's place the first player marker in the center of the table. This is where panes will accumulate when players draft from factories. Make sure everyone can see their floor line at the bottom of their palace board, because that is where excess panes go as penalties.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Let's pick a starting player. The most recent person to visit a cathedral goes first. If nobody has a good answer, just pick randomly. The first player marker will move around as the game progresses.

summary.title: Choose Your Palace Boards
bullets: Each player takes a palace board + 8 double-sided strips (A/B)
First game: use all A sides for consistency

summary.title: Arrange Your Strips
bullets: Slide 8 strips into palace board, left to right (1-8)
Each strip shows which colors can be placed there

summary.title: Place Your Glazier Pawn
bullets: Glazier starts on leftmost strip
Moves right only — never left

summary.title: Set Up Factory Displays
bullets: 2 players → 5 factories
3 players → 7 factories
4 players → 9 factories

summary.title: Fill the Tile Bag
bullets: 100 glass panes total — 20 each of 5 colors
Place all in the bag and mix

summary.title: Stock the Factories
bullets: Draw 4 random panes onto each factory display
Remaining panes stay in the bag

summary.title: Prepare the Center and Floor Line
bullets: Place 1st-player marker in the center area
Floor line at bottom of palace board — holds excess panes as penalties

summary.title: Determine First Player
bullets: Most recent cathedral visitor goes first
1st-player marker shifts each round

walkthrough.title: Round Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's look at how a round works. Each round has two phases: the drafting phase where everyone takes turns picking glass panes, and the scoring phase where completed strips are scored and flipped. Rounds repeat until someone has broken all eight of their strips or the bag runs out of panes.

walkthrough.title: Drafting from a Factory
walkthrough.text: Let's learn the core action. On your turn, pick one factory display and choose one color of pane from it. Take all panes of that color from that factory. The remaining panes on that factory get pushed to the center of the table. This is exactly like drafting in the original Azul.

walkthrough.title: Drafting from the Center
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the center. Instead of taking from a factory, you may take all panes of one color from the center pile. If you are the first player to take from the center this round, you also take the first player marker, which unfortunately counts as a pane on your floor line.

walkthrough.title: Moving Your Glazier
walkthrough.text: Let's place the panes you just drafted. Move your glazier to the right to any strip that accepts the color you drafted. The glazier can skip strips, but it can never move left. Place as many drafted panes as that strip can hold. Any panes that do not fit go to your floor line.

walkthrough.title: Floor Line Penalties
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about what happens to excess panes. Any drafted panes you cannot place on a strip go onto your floor line. Each space on the floor line shows a penalty value that increases as it fills up. The first couple of spaces are small penalties, but later spaces cost you dearly.

walkthrough.title: Completing and Scoring a Strip
walkthrough.text: Let's see what happens when you fill every space on a strip. That strip is scored immediately based on which round you completed it in. Strips completed in earlier rounds score more points. Then you flip the strip over to its broken glass side, meaning it can never receive panes again.

walkthrough.title: Glazier Reset
walkthrough.text: Let's cover what happens when your glazier runs out of room. If your glazier is forced past the rightmost unbroken strip, it resets back to your leftmost unbroken strip. This lets you start working through your strips again from the left side.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Cleanup
walkthrough.text: Let's wrap up the round. Once all panes have been drafted from all factories and the center, the round ends. Subtract any floor line penalties from your score. Then clear the floor line, refill all factories with four new panes each from the bag, and begin a new round.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about when the game ends. The game ends at the conclusion of the round in which any player has broken all eight of their palace strips. It can also end if there are not enough panes in the bag to refill all the factories.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Let's count up the final bonuses. You earn bonus points for completing full sets of one color across all your strips. You also earn a bonus for having the fewest broken panes on your floor line across the whole game. Add these to your running score to determine the winner.

summary.title: Round Overview
bullets: Draft phase → all players take turns picking panes
Score phase → completed strips scored and flipped
Repeat until someone breaks all 8 strips or bag empties

1st player to take from center → takes 1st-player marker (floor line penalty)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Workshop
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through the first few turns of a three-player game so you can see how drafting, glazier movement, and strip completion work in practice. We have three players: Alice, Ben, and Clara. All seven factory displays are loaded with four random panes each, and everyone's glazier starts on their leftmost strip.

walkthrough.title: Alice Drafts Carefully
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Alice take the first turn. She looks at all seven factories and finds one with two blue panes. Her first strip accepts blue, so she takes both blue panes from that factory and places them on strip one. The other two panes from that factory slide to the center. Clean and efficient with no waste.

walkthrough.title: Ben Makes a Big Grab
walkthrough.text: Let's see what Ben does. He spots a factory with three red panes and grabs them all. His first strip only accepts one red pane, so he places one and the other two spill onto his floor line. That is two penalty spaces filled already on the very first turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Match Draft Size to Strip Capacity
walkthrough.text: Let's learn from Ben's mistake. Before you draft, count how many spaces your target strip has open and compare that to how many panes you are about to take. Drafting more panes than your strip can hold means automatic floor line penalties. Sometimes taking fewer panes of a color from a smaller factory is smarter than grabbing a big haul you cannot use.

walkthrough.title: Clara's Opening Move
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Clara think ahead. She takes two yellow panes from a factory and moves her glazier to strip two, which accepts yellow and has exactly two spaces. Both panes fit perfectly. She also notices that strip three accepts the same color that just got pushed to the center, setting herself up for next turn.

walkthrough.title: Ben Pushes His Glazier Too Far Right
walkthrough.text: Let's come back to Ben on his second turn. He drafts green panes but his first and second strips do not accept green. He has to move his glazier all the way to strip five to find a green strip. That means he skipped strips two, three, and four entirely. Those strips are now behind his glazier and he cannot go back to them until the glazier resets.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Do Not Rush Right Too Quickly
walkthrough.text: Let's understand why Ben's move was costly. Moving the glazier far to the right early in the game leaves strips on the left unfilled. Those strips are essentially wasted until your glazier resets, and resetting only happens when you run past the rightmost unbroken strip. Plan your drafts so you can fill strips in order, working left to right at a steady pace.

walkthrough.title: Alice Completes Her First Strip
walkthrough.text: Let's see a strip completion. Alice drafts one more blue pane and fills the last space on strip one. Since this is round one, she scores the maximum point value for that strip. The strip flips over to its broken glass side. Alice's glazier is now sitting on a completed strip and will need to move right on her next draft.

walkthrough.title: Clara Eyes the Center
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Clara draft from the center. Several panes have accumulated there from earlier factory drafts. Clara takes three orange panes from the center and moves her glazier one strip to the right to strip three, which accepts orange. She places two panes and the third goes to her floor line. She also picks up the first player marker, adding one more penalty.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Color Requirements Before Drafting
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about Clara's small mistake. She drafted orange without confirming her target strip had three open spaces. Always scan your accessible strips before choosing a color. Look at every strip to the right of your glazier and check which colors they accept and how many spaces remain. Drafting a color that does not match any reachable strip means all of those panes go straight to the floor line.

walkthrough.title: Ben Recovers With a Reset
walkthrough.text: Let's see Ben's glazier reset in action. Ben fills his fifth strip and then fills strips six and seven over the next two turns. With only strip eight remaining and his glazier sitting on strip seven, his next move pushes the glazier past the last strip. The glazier resets to his leftmost unbroken strip, which is strip two. Now Ben can finally work on those strips he skipped earlier.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan the Reset, Do Not Stumble Into It
walkthrough.text: Let's reframe the reset. Ben eventually recovered, but he wasted several turns with suboptimal placements just to reach the right side. A better approach is to plan your reset deliberately. Fill strips on the right side when the colors line up naturally, and use the reset as a strategic tool to cycle back to unfinished left-side strips at the right time.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's wrap up the round. All panes have been drafted. Alice scored well from completing a strip early with minimal floor penalties. Ben lost points from floor penalties and skipped strips. Clara sits in the middle with a solid position but a few avoidable penalties. The factories are refilled and round two begins.

walkthrough.title: Putting It All Together
walkthrough.text: Let's review the three key lessons from this tutorial. First, match your draft size to your strip capacity so you avoid floor penalties. Second, do not rush your glazier to the right because skipping strips wastes scoring opportunities. Third, always check which colors your accessible strips accept before you commit to a draft.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text: Before you start your own game, check out General Tips for more strategic guidance and Advanced Strategies for scoring optimization. You can also ask your personal GM Guide any question during play by tapping the Q&A tab. Good luck building your stained glass masterpiece.

Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Fill Strips Left to Right
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the most fundamental strategy. Since your glazier can only move right, try to fill strips roughly in order from left to right. This maximizes the number of strips you can reach on any given turn. Skipping strips early means you cannot come back to them without a full reset cycle.

walkthrough.title: Count Before You Draft
walkthrough.text: Let's build a simple habit. Before you take panes from a factory or the center, count how many you are about to pick up and compare that to the open spaces on your target strip. Taking three panes when your strip only holds one means two panes go straight to your floor line as penalties.

walkthrough.title: Score Strips Early for Maximum Points
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about timing. Completing strips in earlier rounds earns you more points than completing them later. Prioritize filling strips that are close to completion rather than spreading your panes across many half-finished strips. One completed strip is worth far more than three strips with a single pane each.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Opponents Need
walkthrough.text: Let's add some table awareness. Pay attention to what colors your opponents are collecting and which strips they are working on. If you can draft a color that helps you while also denying it to an opponent, that is a double win. Hate-drafting can be especially powerful in two-player games.

walkthrough.title: Manage the Center Carefully
walkthrough.text: Let's discuss center timing. Taking from the center gives you access to a larger selection of panes, but the first person to do so takes the first player marker penalty. Sometimes it is worth letting an opponent take the penalty to go first next round while you wait for a better factory option.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Glazier Resets
walkthrough.text: Let's think about the reset as a strategic tool. When your glazier has passed most of your strips, consider which unbroken strips remain on the left. A well-timed reset lets you cycle back to fill those early strips with fresh drafts. Do not wait until you are forced to reset with nothing useful to come back to.

walkthrough.title: Avoid Drafting Dead Colors
walkthrough.text: Let's prevent the most common beginner mistake. Before every draft, scan all the strips to the right of your glazier. If none of them accept the color you are eyeing, every single pane of that color will land on your floor line. Always confirm that at least one reachable strip matches the color you plan to take.

walkthrough.title: Keep Floor Line Penalties Minimal
walkthrough.text: Let's respect the floor line. The penalty values escalate quickly as you fill more spaces. One or two panes on the floor is manageable, but five or six can erase an entire strip completion worth of points. Treat your floor line as something to actively avoid, not just a cost of doing business.

summary.title: Fill Strips Left to Right
bullets: Glazier moves right only — fill strips roughly in order
Skipping strips wastes them until the next reset cycle
Steady left-to-right pace maximizes scoring opportunities

Excess panes → floor line penalties

summary.title: Keep Floor Line Penalties Minimal

walkthrough.title: Optimize Strip Completion Order
walkthrough.text: Let's think about which strips to target first. Since strips completed in earlier rounds score more points, identify which strips have the fewest spaces and require the most common colors. Complete those small, easy strips in rounds one and two to maximize the early-round scoring bonus.

walkthrough.title: Use A/B Strip Selection Strategically
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about strip sides. The A and B sides of each strip show different color requirements. Before the game begins, study both sides and choose a configuration that creates natural color runs where adjacent strips share required colors. This lets you use leftover panes from one strip on the next without wasting drafts.

walkthrough.title: Deliberate Reset Timing
walkthrough.text: Let's master the reset. Advanced players intentionally complete right-side strips early to trigger a fast reset. By quickly filling strips six through eight, you reset your glazier to the left and get a second pass through strips that are now partially filled. This doubles your scoring windows within the same game.

walkthrough.title: Chase End-Game Color Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Let's plan for the finish. End-game bonuses for completing full color sets can swing the final standings dramatically. Track which colors you have placed across all your strips and focus your late-game drafts on completing sets. A single color bonus can be worth more than an entire strip completion in the final round.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Drafting and Denial
walkthrough.text: Let's weaponize the draft. Watch which colors your opponents need and time your picks to deny them critical panes. If an opponent needs two blue panes to complete a strip in round one, taking those blues first forces them into a less efficient turn. Defensive drafting is most impactful when you can use the panes yourself rather than wasting them on your floor line.

walkthrough.title: Center Pile Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Let's control the center. When you draft from a factory, the leftover panes go to the center. Pay attention to what you are sending there. Ideally, push colors to the center that you want and your opponents do not. Then draft from the center on a later turn when it has accumulated the exact colors you need at minimal penalty cost.

walkthrough.title: Minimize Total Floor Exposure
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about floor line management across the whole game. Track your cumulative floor penalties over all rounds, not just one. Advanced players aim for zero to two floor panes per round across the entire game. If you can keep your total game floor penalties under eight points, you are playing at a high level.

walkthrough.title: Read the Factory Landscape
walkthrough.text: Let's look at the big picture before each draft. Before your turn, scan every factory and the center to understand the full landscape of available colors. Identify not just what you want now, but what will be available to you next turn based on what opponents are likely to take. Thinking two drafts ahead separates good players from great ones.

summary.title: Optimize Strip Completion Order
bullets: Target strips with fewest spaces + most common colors first
Complete small strips in rounds 1-2 for max early-round bonus
Front-load scoring before point values decrease

bullets: Study both sides before the game starts
Choose configs where adjacent strips share required colors
Natural color runs reduce wasted drafts

bullets: Complete right-side strips early → fast glazier reset
Second pass through partially-filled left-side strips
Doubles scoring windows within the same game

summary.title: Chase End-Game Color Bonuses
bullets: Track colors placed across all strips throughout the game
Focus late-game drafts on completing color sets
A single color bonus can outweigh a final-round strip completion

summary.title: Defensive Drafting and Denial
bullets: Watch opponents' target colors and strip needs
Take their critical panes when you can use them too
Most impactful when denial panes also serve your strips

summary.title: Center Pile Manipulation
bullets: Factory leftovers go to center — control what you push there
Push colors you want, opponents don't

summary.title: Minimize Total Floor Exposure

summary.title: Read the Factory Landscape

Identify what's available now AND what will remain after opponents pick
Thinking 2 drafts ahead separates good from great

entries.definition: One of 100 colored tiles representing stained glass pieces. There are 20 panes in each of five colors. Panes are drawn from a bag and placed onto factory displays, then drafted by players to fill their palace strips.

A circular tile in the center of the table that holds exactly four glass panes. The number of factories depends on player count: five for two players, seven for three players, nine for four players. When a player drafts from a factory, leftover panes slide to the center.

Each player's personal board that holds eight palace strips and a floor line. The board has slots where strips slide in from left to right. It also shows the floor line penalty track along the bottom edge.

One of eight double-sided cardboard strips that slide into the palace board. Each strip has spaces for glass panes and shows which colors can be placed on it. When completely filled, the strip is scored and flipped to its broken glass side.

A player's colored pawn that sits on their palace board and indicates which strip they are currently working on. The glazier can only move to the right to reach new strips, never to the left. It resets to the leftmost unbroken strip when forced past the rightmost one.

A token placed in the center at the start of each round. The first player to draft from the center takes this marker, which occupies one floor line space as a penalty. That player becomes the first player next round.

A cloth bag holding all glass pane tiles. At the start of each round, panes are drawn randomly from the bag to fill the factory displays. When the bag runs out, previously discarded panes may be returned to it.

The act of selecting all panes of one color from a single factory display or from the center pile. You must take every pane of the chosen color from your selected source — you cannot take a partial set.

The area in the middle of the table where leftover panes accumulate. When a player drafts from a factory, the unchosen panes slide to the center. Players may draft from the center instead of a factory, but the first to do so each round takes the first player marker penalty.

Intentionally taking panes that an opponent needs, even if they are not ideal for your own strips. Most effective when the drafted panes can also serve your own strips, avoiding floor line waste while denying opponents critical colors.

On each turn, after drafting panes, the glazier moves to the right to a strip that accepts the drafted color. The glazier may skip strips but can never move left. If no strip to the right accepts the color, all panes go to the floor line.

When the glazier is forced past the rightmost unbroken strip, it resets to the leftmost unbroken strip on the palace board. This allows the player to begin filling strips from the left side again. Advanced players time this reset deliberately.

Each palace strip is double-sided. The A side and B side show different color requirements and space configurations. Players choose which side to use during setup, creating variety between games. Mixing A and B sides across strips is allowed.

When a strip is completely filled with panes, it is scored and then flipped to its broken glass side. A flipped strip cannot receive any more panes for the rest of the game. It is permanently completed.

Each strip specifies which colors of glass panes can be placed on it. The spaces on a strip show the accepted color or colors. You can only place panes that match the strip's requirements, and only if your glazier is currently on or has moved to that strip.

Strips completed in earlier rounds score more points than strips completed in later rounds. The exact point values decrease as the game progresses, incentivizing fast strip completion over slow, methodical play.

A penalty track at the bottom of each player's palace board. Excess panes that cannot be placed on a strip go here. Each floor line space shows an increasing penalty value. Penalties are subtracted from the player's score at the end of each round.

An end-game bonus awarded for completing full sets of one color across all palace strips. Players who place panes of one color on every strip that requires it earn additional points. These bonuses can significantly affect final standings.
An end-game bonus awarded to the player with the fewest total floor line penalties accumulated across all rounds. Rewards disciplined drafting and efficient pane placement throughout the entire game.

The first phase of each round where players take turns selecting panes from factory displays or the center. The phase continues until all panes have been drafted from all sources. Turn order is determined by the first player marker.

The second phase of each round, occurring after all panes have been drafted. Completed strips are scored, floor line penalties are subtracted, and the floor line is cleared. Factories are then refilled for the next round.

The game ends at the conclusion of the round in which any player breaks (completes and flips) all eight of their palace strips, or when the tile bag cannot refill all factory displays. Final scoring includes end-game bonuses.
